shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: a bruised, low cloud deck sliding over the garden; the light going green-gray

segments.text: Weather is what the air is doing right now — and all of it, every drop and gust, traces back to a single engine: the sun. Sunlight warms the Earth unevenly, land faster than water, equator more than poles. Warm air rises, cool air sinks to replace it, water evaporates and returns — and that restless churn, forever chasing its own balance, is the weather.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: the grass ocean darkening wave by wave as the cloud shadow crosses it

segments.text: Those clouds are the machinery made visible. As warm, moist air rises it cools, and its invisible water vapor condenses onto specks of dust and salt into countless tiny droplets. A cloud is simply the point where the sky's water becomes too heavy to stay hidden.

shot.subject: rain
shot.notes: the first drop strikes a grass blade dead center, detonating in slow motion

segments.text: And when those droplets collide and merge until they are too heavy to float — the weather arrives.

shot.subject: rain
shot.notes: the full downpour: curtains of streaks sweeping the field

segments.text: Rain is the water cycle closing its loop: water lifted from lake and leaf, carried aloft as vapor, returned to the ground as liquid. Every storm is the sky repaying a loan it took from the world below — and the whole garden drinks through its roots on the strength of that repayment.

shot.subject: rain
shot.notes: at ant height beneath the canopy: drops detonating on soil like shellfire

segments.text: But scale, as ever, is everything. To us a raindrop is a touch. To an ant it is a water balloon dropped from orbit — a body of water many times its own mass, striking at the speed of a fall. The citizens of the floor do not experience drizzle. They experience bombardment, and they engineer their days around it.

shot.subject: worm
shot.notes: worms surfacing onto glistening ground; above, wings gathering

segments.text: We have seen who profits. The worms surface as their tunnels flood, the birds come down to take them, and the wet meadow briefly becomes a market. A storm is a disaster and a windfall at once, depending entirely on where you stand.

shot.subject: underwater
shot.notes: beneath the lake surface: the ceiling hammered into rings of light

segments.text: Even the drowned country feels it — the roof of the fish's world drummed into rings, the same fresh water that fell on the grass now raising the whole lake by a finger's width.

shot.subject: lightning
shot.notes: the dome splits: a jagged bolt from cloud to horizon, forks leaping sideways

segments.text: Then the sky's most violent trick. Inside a tall storm cloud, powerful updrafts fling ice crystals and water droplets past each other, and the collisions strip away electric charge — lighter bits carried up, heavier bits sinking down — until the cloud is split into a charged top and a charged base. When the difference grows too great to hold, it discharges in a single searing stroke.

shot.subject: lightning
shot.notes: freeze on the bolt's jagged geometry, every kink and fork etched

segments.text: That stroke heats the air around it to several times the temperature of the sun's surface, in a fraction of a second. The air cannot get out of the way — it explodes outward, and the shockwave rolling back to us is thunder. Light outruns sound, which is why the flash always comes first and the roll arrives late.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: after-flash darkness; the meadow holding still as thunder rolls through

segments.text: Count the gap between them and you are measuring distance — sound crosses roughly a kilometer every three seconds. The residents cannot count. They only know the oldest rule: when the sky speaks in that voice, be small, and be under something.

shot.subject: snow
shot.notes: another day entirely: fat flakes drifting down through windless silence

segments.text: When the air is cold enough, the sky's water skips the raindrop entirely. Water vapor freezes straight onto a speck into a crystal of ice — and because of the shape of the water molecule itself, that crystal always grows six-sided. Every snowflake, however elaborate, keeps the same six-fold symmetry.

shot.subject: snow
shot.notes: following one flake in its spiral descent from canopy height to the grass tops

segments.text: A flake falls slowly because it is mostly air — a lattice of ice with far more surface than weight. At the ant's scale it drifts down like a slow lace plate, taking its time, negotiating every gust on the way. Thousands descend together, unhurried, as if the sky had all day. It does.

shot.subject: snow
shot.notes: snow settling and holding on soil, stones, and bent grass; white gathering

segments.text: And unlike rain, snow stays — and all that trapped air makes it a blanket. A layer of snow insulates the soil beneath it, holding warmth against the ground, which is how many small creatures and roots survive a hard freeze: not on top of the snow, but sheltered under it.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: the meadow half-transformed: white floor, green towers, gray sky

segments.text: The garden under snow is the garden holding its breath — colors banked, growth paused, the whole small economy waiting out the cold under a white roof until the thaw releases it.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: no rain, no snow: just wind, the whole grass ocean streaming one direction

segments.text: Beneath every named weather runs the unnamed one. Wind is simply air on the move — flowing, always, from where the pressure is high toward where it is low. Warm rising air leaves low pressure behind it; cooler air slides in to fill the gap; and that flow, from the garden's breeze to the planet's great winds, is the sky forever leveling itself.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: following a single gust: a visible wave of bent grass racing across the meadow

segments.text: A gust is the only weather you can watch travel. Pick one up at the far treeline and ride it — a wave of bent grass racing the towers, flowing around the rocks, gone over the rim in seconds. The air is invisible; its signature is everywhere.

shot.subject: butterfly
shot.notes: a butterfly caught crabbing sideways across a gust, correcting, persisting

segments.text: For the fliers, wind is the sea they swim in. The butterfly ferries across a gust like a rower crossing a current; the bee lowers its head and burns more fuel; the birds ride it, spend it, and bank it. No flight in this garden ever goes precisely where its wings point. The wind always countersigns the route.

shot.subject: web
shot.notes: a web bellying in the wind, spider riding it, the whole net straining and holding

segments.text: And the wind does the garden's heavy work: it carries seeds, spreads pollen, and moves the sky's heat and water from where there is too much to where there is too little. Everything flexible bargains with it, hour by hour — the one force that never sits down at the table.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: rising through clearing air: the storm's edge sliding off the world, sun returning

segments.text: Every storm ends the same way: the deck breaks, the lamp returns, and the garden files its claims. The lake is fuller. The soil is darker and softer. And string enough of these days together — the long average of all this weather, year after year — and you have something larger and slower than any storm: you have climate, the pattern the weather draws over decades.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: a bloom shedding its last rainwater, petals springing back one by one

segments.text: Weather takes no sides and keeps no favorites; it simply moves heat and water around a turning world and passes on. The garden has never once been ready for it, and never once failed to recover. That, in the end, is what resilience looks like at any scale.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: post-storm gold: the whole garden gleaming, every kingdom lit at once

segments.text: With the weather, our tour of the garden's stage is complete — land, water, sky, stars, moon, and storm. What remains is the best part: the connections. The stories where one resident's episode collides with another's.

shot.subject: web
shot.notes: a web strung with rain jewels, its owner waiting at the hub

segments.text: We begin where every flier's story was always going to end. Next: Web of Danger.
